narrative:
Eric Sorensen flipped Illinois's 17th Congressional District for Democrats in 2022, becoming the first openly gay person to represent Illinois in Congress and bringing a résumé that has almost nothing to do with politics [1]. Before running for office, Sorensen spent over two decades as a television meteorologist in the Quad Cities, which means he spent his career telling people about storms and then decided to walk directly into one. His district sprawls across western and central Illinois, encompassing the Quad Cities, Peoria, and a patchwork of small towns and farmland — a swing district where John Deere tractors and union cards still matter, and where the previous Republican incumbent, Darin LaHood, left behind a seat that Democrats barely snatched [2]. Sorensen sits on the Agriculture Committee and the Science, Space, and Technology Committee, assignments that make sense for a weather guy representing corn country [3].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Sorensen has positioned himself as a pragmatic populist with a reform streak that plays well in a purple district. He cosponsored the ban on congressional stock trading, a low-risk, high-reward position that lets him distinguish himself from Washington insiders without alienating donors [4]. He has supported Medicare drug price negotiation and voted for the legislative framework that enabled it [5]. He backs paid family leave, the right to repair — an issue that resonates deeply in a farm-equipment district where John Deere's repair monopoly is a kitchen-table grievance — and has spoken in favor of banning junk fees [6][7]. He has also supported the DISCLOSE Act and expressed openness to campaign finance transparency measures [8]. On protecting U.S. farmland from foreign purchases, Sorensen has been vocal, cosponsoring restrictions that align with his Agriculture Committee portfolio and his constituents' real anxieties about Chinese-linked land acquisitions near military installations [9].

Where Sorensen gets quieter is on the reforms that would require him to bite the hands reaching into his campaign coffers. He has not championed a lifetime lobbying ban, has not cosponsored a constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United, and has been cautious on congressional term limits — the kind of issues that his moderate, swing-district instincts tell him to avoid [10]. On auditing the Pentagon, he has been silent, and on taxing the ultra-wealthy, he has not gone further than general statements about "tax fairness" that commit him to nothing specific. The AUMF repeal question is one he has approached gingerly, supporting narrow repeals but not sweeping ones, consistent with his overall brand of careful, don't-scare-the-suburbs centrism.

Follow the money and the caution makes more sense. As a freshman in a competitive district, Sorensen has raised aggressively, pulling in over $3.5 million in his 2024 cycle [11]. His top donors include agricultural interests, labor unions, and leadership PACs from Democratic colleagues invested in holding the seat [12]. The real estate industry and health sector also contribute meaningfully. Corporate PACs have not been shy about supporting him, which may explain why banning corporate PACs has not appeared on his legislative agenda. Sorensen is the kind of Democrat the party recruits for swing districts: personable, noncontroversial, and willing to support reform right up to the point where it might cost him a race.

Give Sorensen credit for this much: he won a genuinely competitive seat and has not retreated into pure caution. His advocacy on right to repair is substantive and specific, rooted in the real frustrations of farmers who cannot fix their own equipment [13]. His work on climate issues, informed by his meteorology background, is more than performative. But on the structural reforms that would change how Washington operates — dark money, lobbying, term limits, the cozy relationship between corporate PACs and the members they fund — Sorensen is still a freshman playing it safe, hoping the weather forecast stays favorable through the next cycle.

Andrea Salinas is a relatively quiet member of Congress who, by Washington standards, is doing something deeply suspicious: she appears to actually mean what she says. The daughter of a Mexican-American farmworker, she represents Oregon's 6th district and has the unsettling habit of introducing legislation that matches her campaign promises — a practice most of her colleagues abandoned sometime around their second fundraiser.

On the congressional stock trading ban, Salinas co-led the No Getting Rich in Congress Act alongside Reps. Haley Stevens, Derek Tran, and Eric Sorensen — sweeping legislation that would prohibit members of Congress, federal officials, and their families from trading individual stocks, futures, commodities, and cryptocurrency. She also signed onto a bipartisan letter urging House leadership to bring stock trading ban legislation to the floor. Salinas does not own individual stocks herself, which in Congress is roughly as unusual as bringing your own lunch to a Michelin-starred restaurant. "Members of Congress, federal officials, and their family members should never be allowed to profit from privileged information," she said — a statement so obviously correct it practically disqualifies her from leadership.

During the 2024 cycle, Salinas raised roughly $5 million for her rematch against Republican Mike Erickson, outraising him by a ratio of about 11 to 1. During her 2022 campaign, she petitioned voters to sign onto a stock trading ban for members of Congress — back when she was still a candidate, not yet an incumbent with a PAC ecosystem to protect.

She serves on the House Agriculture Committee and the House Science, Space, and Technology Committee, and co-chairs the Bipartisan Mental Health Caucus. From her Agriculture seat, she has championed farmworker housing, securing nearly $1 million in USDA funding for affordable housing rehabilitation in McMinnville, Oregon. She helped pass what she describes as the nation's most progressive paid family leave bill during her time in the Oregon state legislature.

On consumer protection, Salinas has gone after delivery app corporations for their predatory fee structures that squeeze small businesses — the sort of junk fees that 80% of Americans despise but that somehow never quite make it to a floor vote. She introduced the Soil CARE Act and the Rural Health Care Facilities Revitalization Act, bills that serve her agricultural district but generate approximately zero cable news segments.

Her attendance record is almost comically good: she missed just 13 of 1,673 roll call votes — a 0.8% miss rate, well below the House median. In an institution where showing up is optional and legislating is discouraged, Salinas treats the job like an actual job. The establishment remains baffled.

Eric Schmitt is Missouri's junior U.S. Senator, a Republican who rode into office in 2022 on a wave of Koch money and culture-war lawsuits. As Missouri's Attorney General, he made headlines suing school districts over mask mandates and the federal government over social media "censorship," earning the adoration of the right-wing media circuit [1][2]. According to GovTrack's 2024 report card, Schmitt joined bipartisan bills less often than virtually any other Senate Republican — a distinction that tells you everything about his approach to the 80% Bill's broadly popular reforms [3].

On paper, Schmitt has endorsed Congressional Term Limits, signing the U.S. Term Limits pledge to support a constitutional amendment capping House members at three terms and senators at two [4]. That is where his alignment with the 80% Bill effectively ends. He has not co-sponsored legislation to Ban Congressional Stock Trading, impose a Lifetime Lobbying Ban, or pass the DISCLOSE Act — three pillars of anti-corruption reform that enjoy overwhelming public support but would crimp the fundraising apparatus that put him in the Senate [5]. On the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare Drug Negotiation provisions, Schmitt mocked the bill as a "socialist boondoggle" and opposed it entirely, siding with the pharmaceutical industry over the 80% of Americans who want lower drug prices [6][7].

Schmitt voted against the Fiscal Responsibility Act of 2023, positioning himself as a spending hawk, yet he has shown no interest in Auditing the Pentagon — the one institution that has failed its audit seven consecutive years and cannot account for trillions in spending [8][9]. On Protecting US Farmland, Schmitt carries a particularly awkward record: as a state senator in 2013, he voted twice for legislation that loosened restrictions on foreign ownership of Missouri farmland, a fact his 2022 opponent hammered relentlessly [10]. He has since tried to rehabilitate his image on the issue but has not co-sponsored the strongest federal proposals to ban foreign agricultural purchases. On Banning Dark Money and Corporate PACs, Schmitt is a direct beneficiary of the system — Americans for Prosperity Action, the Koch network's super PAC, spent over $1.6 million boosting his 2022 campaign, and his top donor sectors include finance, real estate, and oil and gas [11][12][13].

Follow the money and Schmitt's priorities snap into focus. His 2022 Senate race drew heavy outside spending from Koch-aligned groups, with AFP Action making a rare primary endorsement on his behalf [11][12]. His campaign war chest was filled by industries that benefit from blocking Tax the Ultra-Wealthy proposals, closing Tax Loopholes, and passing Right to Repair legislation. On Paid Family Leave, Banning Junk Fees, Funding Social Security, Police Body Cameras, Banning Corporate Purchase of Single Family Homes, and Ending Forever Wars, Schmitt has offered no meaningful support — a wall of silence that speaks louder than any press release [5][14].

The bottom line: Eric Schmitt is the senator who will tweet "Hell yeah, release the Epstein files" one month and then tell Politico he does not "know what they have" the next [15]. He pledges term limits while being the least bipartisan Republican in the Senate. He campaigns against China buying farmland after voting to let them do it. He rails against big government while cashing checks from the biggest corporate donors in American politics. Missouri got a culture warrior when they needed a legislator, and the 80% Bill's twenty common-sense reforms remain exactly where Schmitt's donors want them: nowhere near his desk.

Derek Schmidt arrived in Congress in January 2025 after a career that included a decade in the Kansas state senate and twelve years as Kansas Attorney General. He ran for governor in 2022 with endorsements from Donald Trump, Mike Pence, and Mike Pompeo, but lost narrowly to Democrat Laura Kelly [1]. Undeterred, he pivoted to the open KS-2 House seat in 2024 and won comfortably. A Georgetown Law graduate with a master's from the University of Leicester, Schmidt brings a prosecutorial disposition to his committee assignments on Armed Services, Judiciary, and Small Business [2] -- committees that position him squarely in the Republican mainstream on defense spending, border enforcement, and deregulation.

On the 80% Bill issues, Schmidt's freshman record offers little in the way of surprises and even less in the way of alignment. His official priorities -- border security, tax cuts, and reducing federal bureaucracy -- track the standard GOP playbook with near-perfect fidelity [3]. He has cosponsored the Laken Riley Act and championed immigration enforcement through the Judiciary Committee, but has shown no public interest in the structural reforms that form the core of the 80% Bill: no position on banning congressional stock trading, no support for the DISCLOSE Act or dark money restrictions, no advocacy for term limits, and no enthusiasm for corporate PAC bans. On the Small Business Committee, he has focused on scaling back the SBA rather than, say, tackling the junk fees or corporate consolidation that small businesses actually complain about.

Where Schmidt does brush against 80% Bill territory is on agricultural issues. The Kansas Farm Bureau PAC endorsed him in the 2024 primary, and he has advocated for expanding Kansas agricultural exports through trade frameworks like the 2026 U.S.-India deal [4]. Whether that translates into support for Protect Farmland legislation or Right to Repair -- both of which enjoy broad support among the Kansas farmers he claims to champion -- remains to be seen. On defense, he helped advance the FY2026 NDAA through the Armed Services Committee [5] but has said nothing about the Pentagon's seven consecutive audit failures, suggesting that the money flows out but the accountability does not flow back.

The donor profile tells a story of institutional Republican support. His top campaign contributors from his years in state politics included Koch Industries, the Kansas Association of Realtors, AT&T, Monsanto, and the Community Bankers Association [6]. His 2024 congressional campaign raised $552,762, with the Kansas Farm Bureau and Kansans for Life PACs among his notable backers [7]. These are not the donor profiles of a man likely to champion banning corporate PACs, closing tax loopholes, or taxing the ultra-wealthy -- and nothing in his first year in Congress suggests otherwise.

Derek Schmidt is the kind of freshman representative who does exactly what the party leadership asks and nothing more. He votes reliably, misses almost no roll calls, and issues press releases about border security and military installations. On the twenty issues that unite roughly 80% of Americans -- from stock trading bans to paid family leave to the Epstein files -- his record is a blank page where silence functions as a "no." The question for Kansas's 2nd District voters is whether a representative who is present for every vote but absent on every popular reform is really representing them at all.

Jefferson Shreve arrived in Congress in January 2025 representing Indiana's 6th District, a sprawling swath of east-central Indiana that runs from the Indianapolis suburbs to the Ohio border. Before entering politics, Shreve built his fortune in the self-storage industry as the founder and CEO of Storage Express, which grew into one of the largest privately held self-storage companies in the country with hundreds of facilities across multiple states [1]. He also served one term on the Indianapolis City-County Council. In the 2024 Republican primary, Shreve spent lavishly from his personal wealth — pouring millions of his own dollars into the race — to fend off a crowded field and secure the nomination in a safely red district [2]. He is, in the most literal sense, a businessman-turned-politician who bought his way to the starting line.

On the suite of reforms that enjoy overwhelming public support, Shreve's brief congressional record already reveals the familiar pattern of a Republican freshman who talks about shaking up Washington while quietly falling in line. He has expressed rhetorical support for congressional term limits, a low-cost position for someone who just got there [3]. He has also signaled interest in protecting U.S. farmland from foreign purchases, a popular stance in agricultural Indiana [4]. But on the meatier items — banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, auditing the Pentagon, or closing the carried interest loophole — Shreve has been conspicuously silent. His campaign rhetoric leaned heavily on cutting government spending and reducing the deficit, yet he has shown no appetite for the Pentagon audit that would be the single largest accountability measure available to fiscal hawks.

The financial picture is instructive. Shreve largely self-funded his primary campaign, spending over $3 million of his own money to win the seat [2]. In the general election cycle, his donor base drew heavily from real estate, finance, and construction industries — the sectors that have made him personally wealthy [5]. The National Association of Realtors and affiliated interests were among his supporters, which makes his silence on banning corporate purchases of single-family homes — a policy supported by roughly 80 percent of Americans — rather understandable [6]. He also received support from traditional Republican PACs and leadership funds tied to the party establishment, despite campaigning as an outsider.

Shreve is not an ideologue so much as a dealmaker who applied the logic of the self-storage business to politics: find underserved markets, move fast, and outspend the competition. His constituents in IN-6, many of whom struggle with health care costs, stagnant wages, and the hollowing out of rural communities, might reasonably wonder whether a man whose business model involved renting empty boxes to people has much interest in filling the policy gaps that actually affect their lives. The 80% Bill agenda items — from Medicare drug negotiation to paid family leave to banning junk fees — represent exactly the kind of broadly popular, materially impactful reforms that Shreve has thus far treated as someone else's problem.

Rep. Suhas Subramanyam arrived in Congress in January 2025 as a freshman Democrat with one of the more interesting resumes on Capitol Hill: former Obama White House technology policy advisor, Virginia state legislator, and attorney. The first South Asian American elected to the Virginia General Assembly, he now represents the 10th District — the northern Virginia exurbs of D.C., covering Loudoun, Fauquier, and parts of Prince William and Fairfax counties, where the tech industry and the federal workforce collide. He has been busy, and on some fronts, impressively so.

Subramanyam has distinguished himself early with the LEASH DOGE Act, legislation targeting Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency. The bill would require DOGE to publicly disclose all employees and advisors, their background checks, security clearances, and conflicts of interest. It would mandate weekly updates on a public website detailing federal employee layoffs, funding cuts, and DOGE personnel roles. He has also introduced the Campaign and Election Accountability Act and the No Funds for Forced Labor Act. His attendance record is sterling — missing just 2 of 445 roll call votes (0.4%), better than the vast majority of his colleagues.

On transparency and oversight, Subramanyam talks a good game, and his DOGE legislation suggests he means it. But the 80% Bill demands more than oversight of the latest political villain. Where does Subramanyam stand on banning congressional stock trading? On the DISCLOSE Act? On ending dark money? His campaign raised $199,660 on average, with 74% coming from large individual contributions and 12.56% from PACs — including $37,250 from AIPAC. When nearly three-quarters of your campaign funding comes from large donors, the question is not whether you are corrupt, but whether the system allows you to be independent.

His tech policy background makes him a natural ally for right to repair legislation and junk fee bans, yet these issues have not surfaced prominently in his legislative agenda. In the Virginia legislature, he passed bills lowering tolls, utility costs, and prescription drug prices — populist wins that align with the 80% Bill's spirit. But translating state-level consumer protection into federal action against the lobbying might of the telecom, tech, and pharmaceutical industries is a different beast entirely.

Subramanyam sits on the Science, Space and Technology Committee and the Oversight and Accountability Committee, positions that give him real leverage on government transparency and tech regulation. The question is whether a freshman Democrat bankrolled by large donors and PACs will use that leverage to challenge the system or merely to manage it. His DOGE oversight bills suggest a willingness to fight — but fighting Elon Musk is easy when your party already opposes him. The harder fights are the ones against your own donors: banning corporate PACs, requiring full campaign finance disclosure, and closing the revolving door between Congress and K Street. Those are the fights the 80% Bill demands, and on those, the jury is still out.

Lateefah Simon is, on paper, the kind of representative the 80% Bill was designed for. A MacArthur "Genius" Fellowship recipient at 26, a civil rights organizer since her teens, a single mother who built nationally recognized criminal justice programs alongside then-DA Kamala Harris, and the first legally blind member of Congress -- Simon arrived in Washington in January 2025 carrying the legacy of Barbara Lee's CA-12 seat and a biography that reads like a progressive origin story [1][2]. She serves on the House Oversight and Small Business committees and is a member of the Congressional Progressive Caucus [3].

Simon's alignment with the 80% Bill is remarkably strong across the board. She has pledged to ban congressional stock trading, refuses corporate PAC money, supports ranked-choice voting, and wants to end partisan gerrymandering -- positions that earned her an endorsement from End Citizens United [4][5]. She voted for the Epstein Files Transparency Act and, as a member of the Oversight Committee, called for "full accountability for the survivors who have been denied justice for far too long" [6]. On healthcare, she supports allowing Medicare to negotiate the prices of all drugs and lowering the Medicare eligibility age [7]. She fought House Republicans when they blocked her amendment to protect Medicaid, Medicare, Social Security, and SNAP from cuts [8].

Her criminal justice background gives her particular credibility on policing reform. At BART, where the killing of Oscar Grant motivated her to run for the board, she created the nationally recognized Ambassador Program -- unarmed, de-escalation-trained personnel as an alternative to armed transit police [2][9]. She served as a senior advisor on police reform for Governor Newsom in 2020 [9]. On democracy reform, her campaign website explicitly backs the DISCLOSE Act's goals of transparency and reducing corporate money in elections [5]. She co-sponsored the Lobbyist Loophole Closure Act, taking direct aim at the revolving door between Congress and K Street [10].

Following the money with Simon is relatively straightforward -- and relatively clean. Her 2024 campaign raised approximately $729,595, with no corporate PAC contributions [4][11]. As a freshman representing one of the most progressive districts in America, she has the luxury of a donor base that doesn't demand ideological contortions. Her top endorsers include End Citizens United, Higher Heights for America PAC, and J Street PAC [4][12][13]. The absence of corporate PAC money means she can advocate for taxing the ultra-wealthy and closing the carried interest loophole without the awkward dance of cashing checks from the people she's promising to tax.

The bottom line: Simon is a near-perfect alignment with the 80% Bill's agenda, and she has the receipts -- both legislative and financial -- to back it up. The question isn't whether she supports these policies but whether a freshman in the minority party can move them forward. Her biography suggests she's never let long odds stop her before.

Tim Sheehy is the junior senator from Montana, a former Navy SEAL, and the founder of Bridger Aerospace, an aerial firefighting company whose stock has performed about as well as a water bomber in a headwind. Sheehy defeated longtime Democratic incumbent Jon Tester in 2024 in what became the most expensive Senate race in Montana history, with outside groups pouring nearly $140 million into the contest [1][2]. He arrived in Washington with a net worth estimated at $150 million, a Bronze Star, and the kind of bootstrap mythology that works best when you don't mention the $781,000 in PPP loans your company had forgiven while simultaneously pulling in millions in federal firefighting contracts [3][4].

On the 80% Bill checklist, Sheehy talks a decent game on a few items. He has moved his personal stock holdings into blind trusts and donates his Senate salary, a nod toward the spirit of banning congressional stock trading -- though he has not co-sponsored any such legislation [5]. He deserves credit for teaming up with Elizabeth Warren on a military right-to-repair provision that would let service members fix their own equipment instead of paying defense contractors for the privilege, only to watch the provision get stripped from the final defense bill under intense industry lobbying [6]. He says he supports protecting Social Security and Medicare benefits, pledging to "fight each and every day to honor that promise" -- standard boilerplate that carefully avoids mentioning how he'd actually fund either program [7]. On the Pentagon audit, Sheehy co-authored an op-ed with Warren calling out billions in waste from repair monopolies, though he has stopped well short of demanding the kind of comprehensive audit the department has now failed eight years running [8][9].

Where it gets thin is everywhere else. Sheehy has taken no public position on banning corporate PACs, the DISCLOSE Act, dark money reform, ending gerrymandering, a lifetime lobbying ban, taxing the ultra-wealthy, banning corporate purchases of single-family homes, closing tax loopholes, paid family leave, police body cameras, banning junk fees, or congressional term limits. For a senator who ran on being a political outsider, the silence on campaign finance and ethics reform is conspicuous. He has not signed onto the Epstein Files Transparency Act commission, despite the issue polling through the roof [10]. On foreign ownership of US farmland -- a natural issue for a Montana rancher -- there is no legislative action to point to.

Follow the money and the silence starts to make sense. Sheehy's 2024 campaign was turbocharged by some of the wealthiest men in America. Ken Griffin of Citadel poured $5 million into the "More Jobs, Less Government" super PAC, which spent $12.7 million attacking Tester and another $5.9 million boosting Sheehy [11]. Blackstone CEO Stephen Schwarzman matched Griffin with another $5 million to the same PAC -- and Blackstone employees kicked in $28,519 directly to Sheehy's campaign [12][2]. Americans for Prosperity Action, bankrolled by Koch Industries ($25 million) and the Walton family ($15 million), spent $9 million on the race [11]. Sheehy also collected $109,676 from registered lobbyists [13]. When your campaign is underwritten by billionaires who benefit from the tax code, dark money rules, and deregulation staying exactly as they are, reform bills tend to die quiet deaths in your inbox.

The bottom line on Tim Sheehy is that he is a genuine war hero and a savvy businessman who arrived in the Senate backed by an avalanche of billionaire cash and has governed accordingly. He'll partner with Elizabeth Warren when the optics are good and the defense lobby isn't paying attention, but on the structural reforms that 80% of Americans support -- banning dark money, taxing the ultra-wealthy, ending corporate PAC influence, closing the revolving door -- he is nowhere to be found. Montana got a senator who looks like an outsider and fundraises like an insider.

Tom Tiffany is a Freedom Caucus member who recently discovered that hypocrisy is a renewable resource. In December 2025, Tiffany publicly criticized the "billionaire loophole" flooding Wisconsin elections with unlimited dark money, conveniently forgetting that as a state senator in 2015, he voted for the very law that created it. Wisconsin Act 117 eliminated the $10,000 cap on individual donations to all candidates and political committees, removed limits on contributions to party and legislative campaign committees, and opened the floodgates for billionaire megadonors like Richard Uihlein and Diane Hendricks to pour unlimited cash into state races. Tiffany voted yes. Then he complained about the consequences. This is the legislative equivalent of setting a building on fire and then tweeting about how someone should really call the fire department.

On January 6, 2021, in the hours after a violent mob stormed the Capitol, Tiffany voted to throw out the electoral votes of Arizona and Pennsylvania. He had already signed onto Texas v. Pennsylvania at the Supreme Court in December 2020, attempting to overturn the election results in four states. When the 80% Bill calls for protecting democracy and fair elections, Tiffany is the cautionary tale of what happens when those guardrails are missing.

His donor list reads like a who's who of Wisconsin's billionaire class. The Uihleins, Diane Hendricks of ABC Supply, and the co-founder of Kwik Trip have all written big checks. For a congressman representing the rural timber-and-dairy country of northern Wisconsin, Tiffany's financial backers seem remarkably concentrated among people who have never milked a cow.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Tiffany is a near-perfect zero. He voted for the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which the League of Conservation Voters described as "raising energy costs and providing polluter giveaways to cut taxes for billionaires." He has pushed to delist the gray wolf from endangered species protections to allow hunting seasons, tried to repeal mining moratoriums to clear the way for open-pit mines in northern Wisconsin, and objected to making Juneteenth a federal holiday. He voted against a resolution condemning Russia's abduction of Ukrainian children.

Now Tiffany is running for governor of Wisconsin in 2026, promising to freeze property taxes and implement a "red tape reset" for businesses. His campaign is targeting $40 million in fundraising, which presumably will not come from the dairy farmers and paper mill workers of WI-7. The man who voted to create the billionaire loophole now wants to run the whole state. At least he is consistent: Tiffany has never met a billionaire's agenda he would not champion while claiming to speak for the working class.

Bennie G. Thompson has represented Mississippi's 2nd Congressional District since 1993, making him one of the longest-serving members of the state's delegation. A Democrat in a deep-red state, Thompson has spent over three decades as the ranking member or chair of the House Homeland Security Committee, building a legislative portfolio focused on civil rights, rural development, and keeping the Department of Homeland Security funded and functional [1][2]. He is, by most accounts, the most liberal member Mississippi has sent to Washington in modern history -- which in Mississippi is a bit like being the tallest building in Tupelo.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Thompson checks many of the right boxes. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation -- a provision he championed as far back as H.R. 3, the Lower Drug Costs Now Act, calling it a way to deliver "significant savings for patients, employers and taxpayers" [3][4]. He co-sponsored the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act, which mandated federal body cameras and banned chokeholds [5]. He has been a reliable vote for protecting Social Security, co-sponsoring legislation to keep field offices open and defend benefits from cuts [6]. On farmland, Thompson backed heirs' property reform in the 2018 Farm Bill, a critical issue for Black families in the Delta who had been locked out of USDA programs for generations due to unclear land titles [7].

But Thompson is not a rubber stamp, even for his own party. In a move that stunned Democratic leadership, he was the only House Democrat to vote against H.R. 1, the For the People Act, in 2021 -- the sweeping elections bill that included anti-gerrymandering provisions, campaign finance reform, and the DISCLOSE Act [8]. Thompson explained that his constituents opposed the redistricting portion, fearing independent commissions would dilute minority voting power in a majority-Black district [9]. It was a principled stand, or a convenient one, depending on whom you ask. Either way, it means Thompson voted against fair elections reform, dark money disclosure, and public campaign financing in a single stroke -- three items on the 80% Bill list eliminated with one "nay."

Follow the money and the picture gets more interesting. In the 2024 cycle, Thompson's campaign was funded 65% by PAC contributions, with large individual donations making up another 33% and small donors contributing less than 2% [10]. His top donors include lawyers and law firms, and his committee perch attracts steady contributions from defense contractors, technology firms, and financial services companies -- all industries under Homeland Security Committee jurisdiction [11][12]. It is the classic incumbency fundraising loop: oversee an industry, collect its checks, repeat for thirty years.

The bottom line on Bennie Thompson is a familiar Democratic paradox. He supports popular measures like Medicare drug negotiation, police reform, and Social Security protection, but his lone vote against H.R. 1 -- and the gerrymandering, dark money, and campaign finance reforms it contained -- reveals the limits of even a progressive voting record when self-preservation enters the equation. His PAC-heavy funding model suggests a congressman more comfortable with the system as it exists than with the reforms 80% of Americans say they want.
